audioscript.text: Part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a representative from a train company talking to a woman about her train journey.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 1 to 6.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hello, do you mind if I ask you some questions about your journey today?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: We're doing a customer satisfaction survey.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, OK.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: I've got about 10 minutes before my train home leaves.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: I'm on a day trip.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Great, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So first of all, could you tell me your name?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's Sophie Bird.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: And would you mind telling me what you do?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I'm a journalist.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Oh, really?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That must be interesting.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, it is.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: So, what's the reason for your visit here today?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Work?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Actually, it's my day off.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: I came here to do some shopping.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: All right.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: But I do sometimes come here for work.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now, I'd like to ask some questions about your journey today, if that's OK.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, no problem.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Right, so can you tell me which station you're traveling back to?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Staunfirth, where I live.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Uh. Can I just check the spelling?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: STAUNFIRTH?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. And you traveled from there this morning?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: OK, good.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Next, can I ask what kind of ticket you bought?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I assume it wasn't a season ticket, as you don't travel every day.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: No, I just got a normal return ticket.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: I don't have a rail card, so I didn't get any discount.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: I keep meaning to get one, because it's a lot cheaper.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Yes, you'd have saved 20% on your ticket today.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. So you paid the full price for your ticket.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: I paid £23.70.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: OK, do you think that's good value for money?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Not really, I think it's too much for a journey that only takes 45 minutes.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Yes, that's one of the main complaints we get.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So you didn't buy your ticket in advance?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, I know it's cheaper if you buy a week in advance, but I didn't know I was coming then.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: I know, you can't always plan ahead. So, did you buy it this morning?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: No, it was yesterday.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Right, and do you usually buy your tickets at the station?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I do usually, but the ticket office closes early, and I hate using ticket machines.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: I think ticket offices should be open for longer hours.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: There's always a queue for the machines, and they're often out of order.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. A lot of customers are saying the same thing.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So to answer your question, I got an e-ticket online.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the conversation, you have some time to look at questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 7 to 10.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, thank you.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now I'd like to ask you about your satisfaction with your journey.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: So what would you say you were most satisfied with today?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, I like the Wi-Fi on the train.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: It's improved a lot.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: It makes it easier for me to work if I want to.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: That's the first time today anyone's mentioned that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: It's good to get some positive feedback on that.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Hmm.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: And is there anything you weren't satisfied with?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, normally the trains run on time and are pretty reliable, but today there was a delay.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: The train was about 15 minutes behind schedule.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Hmm. OK, I'll put that down.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Now, uh, I'd also like to ask about the facilities at this station.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: You've probably noticed that the whole station's been upgraded.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: What are you most satisfied with?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh. I think the best thing is that they've improved the amount of information about train times et cetera that's given to passengers.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: It's much clearer, before there was only one board, and I couldn't always see it properly, which was frustrating.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: That's good, and is there anything you're not satisfied with?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Let's see, I think things have generally improved a lot.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: The trains are much more modern, and I like the new cafe, but one thing is that there aren't enough places to sit down.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Especially on the platforms.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: OK, so I'll put seating down, shall I, as the thing you're least satisfied with?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, OK.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: Can I ask your opinion about some of the other facilities?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Uh huh.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: We'd like feedback on whether people are satisfied.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Dissatisfied or neither satisfied nor dissatisfied.
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: OK, uh, what about the parking at the station?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Well, to be honest, I don't really have an opinion, as I never use it.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: So neither satisfied nor dissatisfied for that then?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: Yes, I suppose so.
audioscript.speaker: SOPHIE

audioscript.text: OK, uh, and what about these?
audioscript.speaker: MAN

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 1.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear the chair of a committee on park facilities, addressing her local town council about changes that have been made to the park.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 11 to 16.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: As chair of the Town Council Subcommittee on Park Facilities, I'd like to bring you up to date on some of the changes that have been made recently to the Croft Valley Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So if you could just take a look at the map I handed out, let's begin with a general overview.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So, the basic arrangement of the park hasn't changed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It still has two gates, north and south, and a lake in the middle.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The cafe continues to serve an assortment of drinks and snacks.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And is still in the same place, looking out over the lake, and next to the old museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're hoping to change the location of the toilets and bring them nearer to the center of the park, as they're a bit out of the way at present, near the adventure playground.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In the corner of your map.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The formal gardens have been replanted, and should be at their best in a month or two.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They used to be behind the old museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we've now used the space near the south gate, between the park boundary and the path that goes past the lake towards the old museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We have a new outdoor gym for adults and children, which is already proving very popular.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's by the glass houses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Just to the right of the path from the south gate, you have to look for it, as it's a bit hidden in the trees.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One very successful introduction has been our skateboard ramp.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's in constant use during the evenings and holidays.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Near the old museum, at the end of a little path that leads off from the main path between the lake and the museum.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We've also introduced a new area for wild flowers to attract bees and butterflies.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's on a bend in the path.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That goes round the east side of the lake, just south of the adventure playground.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the presentation, you have some time to look at questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now, let me tell you a bit more about some of the changes to Croft Valley Park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: One of our most exciting developments has been the adventure playground.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We were aware that we had nowhere for children to let off steam, and decided to use our available funds to set up a completely new facility in a large space.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: To the north of the park.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: It's open year round, though it closes early in the winter months, and entrance is completely free.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Children can choose whatever activities they want to do, irrespective of their age.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But we do ask adults not to leave them on their own there.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: There are plenty of seats where parents can relax and keep an eye on their children at the same time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Lastly, the glass houses.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: A huge amount of work has been done on them to repair the damage following the disastrous fire that recently destroyed their western side.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Over £80,000 was spent on replacing the glass walls and the metal supports, as well as the plants that had been destroyed, although unfortunately the collection of tropical palm trees has proved too expensive to replace up to now.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: At present, the glass houses are open from 10 am to 3 pm, Mondays to Thursdays, and it's hoped to extend this to the weekend soon.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: We're grateful to all those who helped us by contributing their time and money to this achievement.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The gardens have really been...
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen and answer questions 17 to 20.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now, let me tell you a bit more about some of the changes to Croft Valley Park.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: One of our most exciting developments has been the adventure playground.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We were aware that we had nowhere for children to let off steam, and decided to use our available funds to set up a completely new facility in a large space.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: To the north of the park.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: It's open year round, though it closes early in the winter months, and entrance is completely free.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Children can choose whatever activities they want to do, irrespective of their age.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But we do ask adults not to leave them on their own there.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: There are plenty of seats where parents can relax and keep an eye on their children at the same time.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Lastly, the glass houses.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: A huge amount of work has been done on them to repair the damage following the disastrous fire that recently destroyed their western side.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Over £80,000 was spent on replacing the glass walls and the metal supports, as well as the plants that had been destroyed, although unfortunately the collection of tropical palm trees has proved too expensive to replace up to now.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: At present, the glass houses are open from 10 am to 3 pm, Mondays to Thursdays, and it's hoped to extend this to the weekend soon.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: We're grateful to all those who helped us by contributing their time and money to this achievement.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The gardens have really been...
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 2.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: What does the speaker say about the adventure playground?
options: Children must be supervised.
It costs more in winter.
Some activities are only for younger children.
No payment is required.
It was recently expanded.

body.title: What does the speaker say about the glass houses?
options: They are closed at weekends.
Volunteers are needed to work there.
They were badly damaged by fire.
More money is needed to repair some of the glass.
Visitors can see palm trees from tropical regions.

audioscript.text: Part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear two students of social history called Annie and Jack discussing their presentation about refrigeration.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You have some time to look at questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 21 to 24.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: OK, Jack.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Before we plan our presentation about refrigeration, let's discuss what we've discovered so far.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Fine, Annie.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Though I have to admit I haven't done much research yet.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Nor me.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: But I found an interesting article about ice houses.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I'd seen some 18th- and 19th-century ones here in the UK, so I knew they were often built in a shady area.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Or underground, close to lakes that might freeze in the winter.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Then blocks of ice could be cut and stored in the icehouse.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: But I didn't realize that insulating the blocks with straw or sawdust meant they didn't melt for months.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: The ancient Romans had refrigeration too.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I didn't know that.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Yes, pits were dug in the ground, and snow was imported from the mountains.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Even though they were at quite a distance, the snow was stored in the pits.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Ice formed at the bottom of it, both the ice and the snow were then sold.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: The ice cost more than the snow, and my guess is that only the wealthy members of society could afford it.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I wouldn't be surprised.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I also came across an article about modern domestic fridges.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Several different technologies are used, but they were too complex for me to understand.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: You have to wonder what happens when people get rid of old ones.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: You mean because the gases in them are harmful for the environment?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Exactly.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: At least there are now plenty of organizations that will recycle most of the components safely, but of course some people just dump old fridges in the countryside.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: It's hard to see how they can be stopped unfortunately.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: In the UK, we get rid of 3 million a year altogether.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That sounds a lot, especially because fridges hardly ever break down.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: That's right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: In this country, we keep domestic fridges for 11 years on average.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And a lot last for 20 or more.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: So if you divide the cost by the number of years you can use a fridge, they're not expensive compared with some household appliances.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: True.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I suppose manufacturers encourage people to spend more by making them different colors and designs.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I'm sure when my parents bought their first fridge, they had hardly any choice.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Yes, there's been quite a change.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Before you hear the rest of the discussion, you have some time to look at questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen and answer questions 25 to 30.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Right, let's make a list of topics to cover in our presentation, and decide who's going to do more research on them.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Then later we can get together and plan the next step.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: OK, how about starting with how useful refrigeration is, and the range of goods that are refrigerated nowadays?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Because of course, it's not just food and drinks.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: No, I suppose flowers and medicines are refrigerated, too.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: And computers. I could do that.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Unless you particularly want to.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: No, that's fine by me.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: What about the effects of refrigeration on people's health?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: After all, some of the chemicals used in the 19th century were pretty harmful.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: But there have been lots of benefits too, like always having access to fresh food.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Do you fancy dealing with that?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I'm not terribly keen to be honest.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Hmm, nor me.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: My mind just goes blank when I read anything about chemicals.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Oh, all right then, I'll do you a favor, but you owe me, Jack.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: What about the effects on food producers, like farmers in poorer countries being able to export their produce to developed countries?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Something for you maybe?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I don't mind, it should be quite interesting.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I think we should also look at how refrigeration has helped whole cities, like Las Vegas, which couldn't exist without refrigeration, because it's in the middle of a desert.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Right.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I had a quick look at an economics book in the library that's got a chapter about this sort of thing.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I could give you the title if you want to do this section.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Not particularly to be honest.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I find economics books pretty heavy going as a rule.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK, leave it to me then.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Then there's transport, and the difference that refrigerated trucks have made.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: I wouldn't mind having a go at that.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Don't forget trains too.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I read something about milk and butter being transported in refrigerated railroad cars in the USA, right back in the 1840s.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: I hadn't thought of trains. Thanks.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: Should we have a separate section on domestic fridges?
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: After all, they're something everyone's familiar with.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: What about splitting it into two?
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: You could investigate 19th and 20th century fridges, and I'll concentrate on what's available these days, and how manufacturers differentiate their products from those of their competitors.
audioscript.speaker: ANNIE

audioscript.text: OK, that suits me.
audioscript.speaker: JACK

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have 30 seconds to check your answers to part 3.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

items.title: What did Annie discover from reading about icehouses?
options: why they were first created
how the ice was kept frozen
where they were located

items.title: What point does Annie make about refrigeration in ancient Rome?
options: It became a commercial business.
It used snow from nearby.
It took a long time to become popular.

items.title: In connection with modern refrigerators, both Annie and Jack are worried about
options: the complexity of the technology.
the fact that some are disposed of irresponsibly.
the large number that quickly break down.

items.title: What do Jack and Annie agree regarding domestic fridges?
options: They are generally good value for money.
There are plenty of useful variations.
They are more useful than other domestic appliances.

text: Who is going to do research into each topic?
textReadable: Who is going to do research into each topic?

test_text.title: How industrial revolution affected life in Britain

audioscript.text: Part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You will hear a history student giving part of a presentation about changes in British society in the 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: Hi, everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In this session, I'll be presenting my research about the social history of Britain during the Industrial Revolution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: I particularly looked at how ordinary lives were affected by changes that happened at that time.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was a time that saw the beginning of a new phenomenon, consumerism, where buying and selling goods became a major part of ordinary people's lives.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, it was in the 19th century that the quantity and quality of people's possessions was used as an indication of the wealth of the country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before this, the vast majority of people had very few possessions, but all that was changed by the Industrial Revolution.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This was the era from the mid 18th to the late 19th century, when improvements in how goods were made, as well as in technology, triggered massive social changes that transformed life for just about everybody in several key areas.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: First, let's look at manufacturing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: When it comes to manufacturing, we tend to think of the Industrial Revolution in images of steam engines and coal.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And it's true that the Industrial Revolution couldn't have taken place at all, if it weren't for these new sources of power.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: They marked an important shift away from the traditional watermills and windmills that had dominated before this.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The most advanced industry for much of the 19th century was textiles.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: This meant that fashionable fabrics and lace and ribbons were made available to everyone.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Before the Industrial Revolution, most people made goods to sell in small workshops, often in their own homes.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But enormous new machines were now being created.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That could produce the goods faster, and on a larger scale, and these required a lot more space, so large factories were built.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Replacing the workshops and forcing workers to travel to work.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: In fact, large numbers of people migrated from villages into towns as a result.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: As well as manufacturing, there were new technologies in transport contributing to the growth of consumerism.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The horse-drawn stagecoaches and carts of the 18th century which carried very few people and goods, and traveled slowly along poorly surfaced roads, were gradually replaced by the numerous canals that were constructed.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: These were particularly important for the transportation of goods.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The canals gradually fell out of use, though, as railways were developed,
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: becoming the main way of moving goods and people from one end of the country to the other, and the goods they moved weren't just coal, iron, clothes, and so on.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Significantly, they included newspapers, which meant that thousands of people were not only more knowledgeable about what was going on in the country.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: But could also read about what was available in the shops, and that encouraged them to buy more.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: So faster forms of transport resulted in distribution becoming far more efficient.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Goods could now be sold all over the country, instead of just in the local market.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The third main area that saw changes that contributed to consumerism was retailing.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The number and quality of shops grew rapidly.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: And in particular, small shops suffered, as customers flocked to the growing number of department stores, a form of retailing that was new in the 19th century.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: The entrepreneurs who opened these found new ways to stock them with goods, and to attract customers.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: For instance, improved lighting inside greatly increased the visibility of the goods for sale.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Another development that made goods more visible from outside resulted from the use of plate glass, which made it possible for windows to be much larger than previously.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: New ways of promoting goods were introduced too.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Previously, the focus had been on informing potential customers about the availability of goods.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Now, there was an explosion in advertising, trying to persuade people to go shopping.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Flanders claims that one of the great effects of the Industrial Revolution was that it created choice, all sorts of things that had previously been luxuries.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: From sugar to cutlery became conveniences, and before long, they turned into necessities.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Life without sugar or cutlery was unimaginable.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: Rather like mobile phones these days.
audioscript.speaker: SPEAKER

audioscript.text: That is the end of part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

audioscript.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
audioscript.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You will hear a history student giving part of a presentation about changes in British society in the 19th century.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: First, you have some time to look at questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: Now listen carefully and answer questions 31 to 40.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before this, the vast majority of people had very few possessions, but all that was changed by the Industrial Revolution.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This was the era from the mid 18th to the late 19th century, when improvements in how goods were made, as well as in technology, triggered massive social changes that transformed life for just about everybody in several key areas.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: First, let's look at manufacturing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: When it comes to manufacturing, we tend to think of the Industrial Revolution in images of steam engines and coal.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And it's true that the Industrial Revolution couldn't have taken place at all, if it weren't for these new sources of power.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: They marked an important shift away from the traditional watermills and windmills that had dominated before this.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The most advanced industry for much of the 19th century was textiles.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: This meant that fashionable fabrics and lace and ribbons were made available to everyone.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Before the Industrial Revolution, most people made goods to sell in small workshops, often in their own homes.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But enormous new machines were now being created.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That could produce the goods faster, and on a larger scale, and these required a lot more space, so large factories were built.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Replacing the workshops and forcing workers to travel to work.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: In fact, large numbers of people migrated from villages into towns as a result.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: As well as manufacturing, there were new technologies in transport contributing to the growth of consumerism.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The horse-drawn stagecoaches and carts of the 18th century which carried very few people and goods, and traveled slowly along poorly surfaced roads, were gradually replaced by the numerous canals that were constructed.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: These were particularly important for the transportation of goods.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The canals gradually fell out of use, though, as railways were developed,
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: becoming the main way of moving goods and people from one end of the country to the other, and the goods they moved weren't just coal, iron, clothes, and so on.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Significantly, they included newspapers, which meant that thousands of people were not only more knowledgeable about what was going on in the country.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: But could also read about what was available in the shops, and that encouraged them to buy more.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: So faster forms of transport resulted in distribution becoming far more efficient.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Goods could now be sold all over the country, instead of just in the local market.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The third main area that saw changes that contributed to consumerism was retailing.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The number and quality of shops grew rapidly.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: And in particular, small shops suffered, as customers flocked to the growing number of department stores, a form of retailing that was new in the 19th century.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: The entrepreneurs who opened these found new ways to stock them with goods, and to attract customers.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: For instance, improved lighting inside greatly increased the visibility of the goods for sale.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Another development that made goods more visible from outside resulted from the use of plate glass, which made it possible for windows to be much larger than previously.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: New ways of promoting goods were introduced too.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Previously, the focus had been on informing potential customers about the availability of goods.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Now, there was an explosion in advertising, trying to persuade people to go shopping.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Flanders claims that one of the great effects of the Industrial Revolution was that it created choice, all sorts of things that had previously been luxuries.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: From sugar to cutlery became conveniences, and before long, they turned into necessities.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Life without sugar or cutlery was unimaginable.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: Rather like mobile phones these days.
utterances.speaker: SPEAKER

utterances.text: That is the end of part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

utterances.text: You now have one minute to check your answers to part 4.
utterances.speaker: ANNOUNCER

body.title: How the Industrial Revolution affected life in Britain
